message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: PREMISE BUSTED: the RACE reference is NOT human-written. It is Gemini-2.5-pro Deep Research output from April 2025. 'Human reference = 0.5000' is false.
DETAIL: Direct quote from the paper (Experimental Setup): 'The reference reports (Rref) used in RACE's scoring methodology were selected from deep research articles generated by the Gemini-2.5-pro-based Deep Research, as available in April 2025.' Corroborated independently by the leaderboards: gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch scores 49.98 (gpt-5.5 judge) and 49.71 (original judge) — i.e. ~0.50, because it is being scored against a sibling sample of its own output. CONSEQUENCE FOR POLARIS: the thing we must beat is a known, imitable, year-old LLM's house style — not a human. Its 59 paragraphs / 142 words-per-para / 24 H3 / 10 tables / 91 bullets is *Gemini DR's* template, and it has known weaknesses (it is the weakest-insight report among all >50 systems). The '0.5000 by construction' intuition is still right (reference vs itself = 0.5), but the 'human' part is wrong and it has been leading us to over-respect the reference.
SYSTEM: RACE-judge-itself
SOURCE GIVEN: https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/static/papers/deepresearch-bench.pdf
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Quote the passage verbatim from the Experimental Setup / Implementation Details section. Also state what judge model was used.

content.content:
# RACE Scoring Methodology - Reference Reports

Based on the PDF content provided, I cannot locate the specific passage from the Experimental Setup or Implementation Details section that explicitly describes whether the reference reports (Rref) were human-written or generated by Gemini-2.5-pro Deep Research.

The document appears to be a research paper on "DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents," but the compressed PDF stream data makes it difficult to extract the precise text describing the reference report generation methodology.

**What I can confirm from the metadata:**
- The paper was authored by Mingxuan Du, Benfeng Xu, Chiwei Zhu, Xiaorui Wang, and Zhendong Mao
- It is available at arXiv.org/abs/2506.11763

**To answer your question accurately, I would need:**
- Access to a readable text version of the Implementation Details section
- The specific subsection describing RACE's scoring methodology and reference report creation

I recommend consulting the original paper directly at the arXiv link or requesting the authors clarify whether reference reports were human-authored or AI-generated for their benchmark evaluation.

[Binary content (application/pdf, 3.5MB) also saved to /home/polaris/.claude/projects/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/tool-results/webfetch-1783919579092-cgxjtr.pdf]

Web search results for query: "DeepResearch Bench RACE reference reports Gemini-2.5-pro Deep Research April 2025"

Links: [{"title":"Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research Insights","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/gemini-2-5-pro-deep-research"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763"},{"title":"FinDeepResearch: Evaluating Deep Research Agents in Rigorous Financial Analysis","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.13936"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents [Quick Review]","url":"https://liner.com/review/deepresearch-bench-comprehensive-benchmark-for-deep-research-agents"},{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"Deep FinResearch Bench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Conduct Professional Financial Investment Research","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.21006"},{"title":"Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover (Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve)","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.20843"},{"title":"ADRA-Bank: A Modular Benchmark for Academic Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.00986"},{"title":"DeepResearchEval: An Automated Framework for Deep Research Task Construction and Agentic Evaluation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.09688"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about DeepResearch Bench, RACE reference reports, and Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research from April 2025:

## Key Findings

**Reference Reports from April 2025:**
The reference reports used in RACE's scoring methodology were selected from deep research articles generated by the Gemini-2.5-pro-based Deep Research, as available in April 2025.

**RACE Evaluation Framework:**
RACE (Reference-based Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation) evaluates comprehensiveness, insight, instruction-following, readability, citation accuracy, and effective citations per task. For RACE evaluation tasks, Gemini-2.5-pro is utilized as the Judge LLM.

**Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research Performance:**
Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research demonstrated leading overall performance (48.88) across all dimensions in the RACE Framework, and achieved an exceptional 111.21 average effective citations, demonstrating superior information gathering capabilities.

**DeepResearch Bench Overview:**
DeepResearch Bench consists of 100 PhD-level research tasks, each meticulously crafted by domain experts across 22 distinct fields.

The search results indicate that the DeepResearch Bench is a comprehensive benchmark that evaluated multiple deep research agents, with Gemini-2.5-Pro performing particularly well on the RACE evaluation metrics.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Quote any passage about the reference reports (R_ref) used in RACE scoring — how were they obtained? Human experts or Gemini-2.5-pro Deep Research? Also what judge LLM is used?

List the leaderboard: what judge model is used now, what score does gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch get, and is there any note about the reference reports being human-written or Gemini-generated? Report all overall scores you see and any mention of a GPT-5.5 judge or updated evaluator.

content.content:
I cannot provide the information you're requesting based on the provided webpage content. The abstract and metadata shown do not contain details about how reference reports (R_ref) were obtained for RACE scoring, nor do they specify which judge LLM was used in the evaluation methodology.

To find these specifics, you would need to access the full paper PDF, which is available at the link provided on the arXiv page.

# DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard Information

**Judge Model:** Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview is the selected judge LLM, chosen for its balance of performance and cost-effectiveness at "$0.13 per query."

**Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research Score:** The model achieved an overall RACE score of 48.88, with particularly strong results across dimensions (Comprehensiveness: 48.53, Depth: 48.50, Instruction-Following: 49.18). It also attained 111.21 effective citations under the FACT framework.

**Reference Reports Note:** The document does not specify whether reference reports used in evaluation were human-written or Gemini-generated.

**Overall Scores Reported:**
- Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research: 48.88 (RACE)
- OpenAI Deep Research: 46.98 (RACE)
- Perplexity Deep Research: 42.25 (RACE)
- Grok Deeper Search: 40.24 (RACE)

**GPT-5.5 Mention:** There is no reference to a GPT-5.5 judge or evaluator in the provided content. The document discusses Claude, Gemini, o3, and o4-mini as Judge LLMs tested within the RACE framework.

input.prompt: What does the README say about the leaderboard, the judge model (any update to GPT-5.5 or other), and about the reference reports used in RACE? Report gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch's score(s) and any 2026 updates.

content.content:
# DeepResearch Bench: Leaderboard, Judge Models, and RACE Updates

## Leaderboard Access
The README directs users to the "[**DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard**](https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard)" for "real-time updated evaluation results, detailed comparative analysis, and raw data."

## Judge Model Updates (2026)
A major update occurred in May 2026. The benchmark "switched" its official evaluator from Gemini-2.5-Pro to **GPT-5.5** for RACE evaluation and **GPT-5.4-mini** for FACT evaluation. This change followed Google's announced deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro on June 17, 2026.

The README states: "GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS" among candidate evaluators tested, achieving an "Overall" score of 71.82% against a human inter-annotator baseline of 68.78%.

## Reference Reports in RACE
The documentation does not specify details about reference reports. RACE methodology involves "compares target reports against high-quality reference reports," but the README provides no additional information about their construction or source.

## Gemini-2.5-Pro Results
**No specific scores** for Gemini-2.5-Pro-DeepResearch appear in the provided content. The README mentions evaluation results exist on the Hugging Face leaderboard but doesn't reproduce scores in this README text.

content.text: Judge switch to GPT-5.5 is confirmed. Now the key sentence in the paper itself.

content.text: PyMuPDF is available. Extracting the paper text directly.

input.command:
cd /tmp && python3 -c "
import fitz, re
d = fitz.open('drb.pdf')
txt = ''.join(p.get_text() for p in d)
open('drb.txt','w').write(txt)
print('pages', d.page_count, 'chars', len(txt))
for kw in ['April 2025','reference report','Reference report','R ref','Rref','Judge','judge']:
    for m in re.finditer(re.escape(kw), txt):
        s=max(0,m.start()-320); e=min(len(txt),m.end()+320)
        print('---',kw,'---'); print(txt[s:e].replace(chr(10),' '))
" | head -120

BigCodeBench: Benchmarking Code Generation with Diverse Function Calls and Complex Instructions, April 2025. arXiv:2406.15877 [cs]. 16 A Limitations While DeepResearch Bench and the RACE & FACT evaluation framework offer the first comprehensive assessment of Deep Research Agents, several limitations remain. Benchmark scale. Creating tasks that authentically reflect real research challenges requires substantial expertise and
--- reference report ---
nuine quality variations. To mitigate this, RACE adopts a reference-based scoring strategy. For each task t, a high-quality report Rref is selected as a reference. All generated criteria {cd,k} across all dimensions are aggregated into a comprehensive list Ct. The Judge LLM then analyzes the target report Rtgt and the reference report Rref against each criterion c ∈Ct. This yields lists of scores for both reports for each criterion, which are then used for final score calculation: ({stgt,c}c∈Ct, {sref,c}c∈Ct) = JudgeLLM(t, Rtgt, Rref, Ct) (2) Overall Score Calculation. Finally, we compute the overall quality score of the target report. First, dimen
--- reference report ---
port. First, dimension-level scores Sd(R) are calculated by weighting criterion-level scores sR,cd,k with criterion weights wd,k. Second, these Sd(R) scores are weighted by dimension weights Wd to produce intermediate overall scores Sint(R). Finally, the target report’s score Sfinal(Rtgt) is determined relative to the reference report’s score: Sfinal(Rtgt) = Sint(Rtgt) Sint(Rtgt) + Sint(Rref) (3) 3.2 FACT: A Framework for Web Retrieval Evaluation To assess the factual grounding of report content and the agent’s effectiveness in retrieving and utilizing web-based information, we introduce the framework for Factual Abundance and Citation Trustworthin
--- reference report ---
 scoring process. For RACE evaluation tasks, we utilize Gemini-2.5-pro as the Judge LLM. As for the FACT framework, Gemini-2.5-flash is employed for both Statement-URL pair extraction and support judgment, which is sufficient in capabilities while more economical for the token-consuming citation verification task. The reference reports (Rref) used in RACE’s scoring methodology were selected from deep research articles generated by the Gemini-2.5-pro-based Deep Research, as available in April 20253. Evaluated Models In our work, we primarily evaluate four early-released Deep Research Agents: Gemini-2.5-pro- based Deep Research, OpenAI Deep Research,
--- Rref ---
coring. Preliminary experiments suggest that scoring reports in isolation often yields insufficiently discriminative results; models tend to assign uniformly high scores, thereby masking genuine quality variations. To mitigate this, RACE adopts a reference-based scoring strategy. For each task t, a high-quality report Rref is selected as a reference. All generated criteria {cd,k} across all dimensions are aggregated into a comprehensive list Ct. --- Rref ---
 are aggregated into a comprehensive list Ct. The Judge LLM then analyzes the target report Rtgt and the reference report Rref against each criterion c ∈Ct. This yields lists of scores for both reports for each criterion, which are then used for final score calculation: ({stgt,c}c∈Ct, {sref,c}c∈Ct) = JudgeLLM(t, Rtgt, Rref, Ct) (2) Overall Score Calculation. Finally, we compute the overall quality score of the target report. First, dimension-level scores Sd(R) are calculated by weighting criterion-level scores sR,cd,k with criterion weights wd,k. Second, these Sd(R) scores are weighted by dimension weights Wd to produce intermediate ove
--- Rref ---
criterion-level scores sR,cd,k with criterion weights wd,k. Second, these Sd(R) scores are weighted by dimension weights Wd to produce intermediate overall scores Sint(R). Finally, the target report’s score Sfinal(Rtgt) is determined relative to the reference report’s score: Sfinal(Rtgt) = Sint(Rtgt) Sint(Rtgt) + Sint(Rref) (3) 3.2 FACT: A Framework for Web Retrieval Evaluation To assess the factual grounding of report content and the agent’s effectiveness in retrieving and utilizing web-based information, we introduce the framework for Factual Abundance and Citation Trustworthiness(FACT). This framework evaluates DRAs through the follo
--- Rref ---
or RACE evaluation tasks, we utilize Gemini-2.5-pro as the Judge LLM. As for the FACT framework, Gemini-2.5-flash is employed for both Statement-URL pair extraction and support judgment, which is sufficient in capabilities while more economical for the token-consuming citation verification task. The reference reports (Rref) used in RACE’s scoring methodology were selected from deep research articles generated by the Gemini-2.5-pro-based Deep Research, as available in April 20253. Evaluated Models In our work, we primarily evaluate four early-released Deep Research Agents: Gemini-2.5-pro- based Deep Research, OpenAI Deep Research, Grok D
--- Judge ---
aches using fixed checklists [23] or static rubrics [25, 1] struggle to adapt to diverse tasks, specialized domains, and nuanced quality aspects of deep research tasks. To address this, we introduce our Reference-based Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation framework with Dynamic Weighting (RACE), leveraging the LLM-as-a-Judge method [36]. RACE offers a more adaptive and robust evaluation by first dynamically generating task-specific weights and criteria. It then employs a reference-based scoring approach, comparing the target report with a high-quality reference. Finally, a relative score is computed to assess the quality of the target rep
--- Judge ---
ed assessment goals. Therefore, we first establish four top-level orthogonal dimensions based on domain expertise: Comprehensiveness (COMP), Insight/Depth (DEPTH), Instruction-Following (INST), and Readability (READ). Detailed definitions are provided in the Appendix B. As illustrated in Figure 2(b), given task t, the Judge LLM derives task-specific dimension weights by averaging weights w(j) d obtained over T trials for each dimension d ∈{COMP, DEPTH, INST, READ}: Wd = 1 T T X j=1 w(j) d (1) These final weights, Wd, ensure that evaluation aligns with the task’s intent. Subsequently, for each dimension d, the Judge LLM generates a set of
--- Judge ---
(b), given task t, the Judge LLM derives task-specific dimension weights by averaging weights w(j) d obtained over T trials for each dimension d ∈{COMP, DEPTH, INST, READ}: Wd = 1 T T X j=1 w(j) d (1) These final weights, Wd, ensure that evaluation aligns with the task’s intent. Subsequently, for each dimension d, the Judge LLM generates a set of Kd tailored criteria {cd,k} and their corresponding weights {wd,k} (where PKd k=1 wd,k = 1), which are clear and actionable for evaluating the report within that dimension. Reference-Based Scoring. Preliminary experiments suggest that scoring reports in isolation often yields insufficiently disc
--- Judge ---
end to assign uniformly high scores, thereby masking genuine quality variations. To mitigate this, RACE adopts a reference-based scoring strategy. For each task t, a high-quality report Rref is selected as a reference. All generated criteria {cd,k} across all dimensions are aggregated into a comprehensive list Ct. The Judge LLM then analyzes the target report Rtgt and the reference report Rref against each criterion c ∈Ct. This yields lists of scores for both reports for each criterion, which are then used for final score calculation: ({stgt,c}c∈Ct, {sref,c}c∈Ct) = JudgeLLM(t, Rtgt, Rref, Ct) (2) Overall Score Calculation. Finally, we co
--- Judge ---
oss all dimensions are aggregated into a comprehensive list Ct. The Judge LLM then analyzes the target report Rtgt and the reference report Rref against each criterion c ∈Ct. This yields lists of scores for both reports for each criterion, which are then used for final score calculation: ({stgt,c}c∈Ct, {sref,c}c∈Ct) = JudgeLLM(t, Rtgt, Rref, Ct) (2) Overall Score Calculation. Finally, we compute the overall quality score of the target report. First, dimension-level scores Sd(R) are calculated by weighting criterion-level scores sR,cd,k with criterion weights wd,k. Second, these Sd(R) scores are weighted by dimension weights Wd to produce
--- Judge ---
nding of report content and the agent’s effectiveness in retrieving and utilizing web-based information, we introduce the framework for Factual Abundance and Citation Trustworthiness(FACT). This framework evaluates DRAs through the following automated steps: Statement-URL Pair Extraction and Deduplication. We employ a Judge LLM to extract discrete statements from reports generated by DRAs with their corresponding cited source URLs. Then, the Judge LLM examines the pairs to identify cases where multiple statements associated with the same URL describe exactly the same fact. In such cases, only one representative Statement-URL pair is reta
--- Judge ---
ework for Factual Abundance and Citation Trustworthiness(FACT). This framework evaluates DRAs through the following automated steps: Statement-URL Pair Extraction and Deduplication. We employ a Judge LLM to extract discrete statements from reports generated by DRAs with their corresponding cited source URLs. Then, the Judge LLM examines the pairs to identify cases where multiple statements associated with the same URL describe exactly the same fact. In such cases, only one representative Statement-URL pair is retained, ensuring each unique factual claim is represented only once. Support Judgment. Each unique Statement-URL pair undergoes 
--- Judge ---
exactly the same fact. In such cases, only one representative Statement-URL pair is retained, ensuring each unique factual claim is represented only once. Support Judgment. Each unique Statement-URL pair undergoes a support evaluation. We retrieve the textual content of the webpage using the Jina Reader API. Then, the Judge LLM assesses whether this content provides sufficient evidence to support the statement. This results in a binary judgment (’support’ or ’not support’) for each pair, determining whether the citation accurately grounds the claim. Calculation of Citation Metrics. Based on these support judgments, we calculate two prima
--- Judge ---

For RACE evaluation tasks, we utilize Gemini-2.5-pro as the Judge LLM. As for the FACT framework, Gemini-2.5-flash is employed for both Statement-URL pair extraction and support judgment, which is sufficient in capabilities while more economical for the token-consuming citation verification task
--- Judge ---
Setup Implementation Details When employing the RACE framework, an initial pre-processing step involves cleaning citation formatting from the generated reports, as overly lengthy or complex citation styles can adversely affect the Judge LLM’s scoring process. For RACE evaluation tasks, we utilize Gemini-2.5-pro as the Judge LLM. As for the FACT framework, Gemini-2.5-flash is employed for both Statement-URL pair extraction and support judgment, which is sufficient in capabilities while more economical for the token-consuming citation verification task. The reference reports (Rref) used in RACE’s scoring methodology were selected from deep
--- Judge ---
-Pro Deep Research demonstrates leading overall performance on the RACE framework. OpenAI Deep Research also exhibits strong capabilities, surpassing Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research in the Instruction-Following dimension. This indicates that the different dimensions are somewhat decoupled and that a sufficiently powerful Judge LLM can capture performance variations across these various dimensions. The scores for these top agents are relatively close, which is characteristic of the reference-based relative scoring employed by 3We cannot confirm if there were any Deep Research model iterations during the reference collection period. 6 Figure 
--- Judge ---
 Evaluation Methods Given that existing evaluation methods are generally unsuitable for assessing deep research reports, our experiments focus on comparing the human consistency of several RACE variants against a vanilla prompt baseline. We evaluate the following methods: RACE (Full), Vanilla Prompt (direct scoring by Judge LLM), ablation variants, Reverse Position (swapping reference and target report positions), and Static Criteria (using fixed criteria). As shown in Table 2, RACE(Full) exhibits the strongest overall performance, significantly outperforming both the vanilla baseline and other 8 RACE variants. Notably, its Pairwise Agre
--- Judge ---
 and other 8 RACE variants. Notably, its Pairwise Agreement Rate also surpasses the inter-agreement rate observed among human experts. These results collectively demonstrate that RACE can reliably and accurately evaluate deep research reports, achieving high human consistency efficiently. 4.3.4 Comparison of Different Judge LLM Leveraging the RACE framework, we further compare the performance and cost of several leading proprietary LLMs when used as the Judge LLM. As detailed in Table 3, Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview not only achieves the best overall performance but also maintains a competitive average cost ($0.13 per query), only higher than 
--- Judge ---
se results collectively demonstrate that RACE can reliably and accurately evaluate deep research reports, achieving high human consistency efficiently. 4.3.4 Comparison of Different Judge LLM Leveraging the RACE framework, we further compare the performance and cost of several leading proprietary LLMs when used as the Judge LLM. As detailed in Table 3, Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview not only achieves the best overall performance but also maintains a competitive average cost ($0.13 per query), only higher than that of o4-mini. Therefore, to effectively balance performance and cost, we select Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview as the Judge LLM in our final fr
--- Judge ---
Ms when used as the Judge LLM. Therefore, to effectively balance performance and cost, we select Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview as the Judge LLM in our final framework. content.text: The paper's exact sentence is confirmed verbatim. Now I must check whether the reference corpus is still Gemini-generated in 2026, and verify the leaderboard numbers.

input.prompt: What is gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch's overall RACE score under the current (GPT-5.5) judge and under the original judge? What judge is used now? Any note about reference reports?

content.content:
I cannot provide the requested information about gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch's RACE scores, the current judge being used, or notes about reference reports based on the webpage content provided.

The page shown is merely a header/navigation interface for a Hugging Face Space called "DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard." It displays "Fetching metadata from the HF Docker repository..." and "Refreshing," indicating the actual leaderboard data hasn't loaded or isn't visible in the provided content. No performance metrics, judge information, or benchmark details are included in what was shared.

To answer your questions, you would need to access the fully-loaded leaderboard page itself.

=== README refs ===
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:16:+ [11 May 2026] 🎯 **Official Evaluator Switched to GPT-5.5**: Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, we benchmarked three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements on the human-annotated subset (50 tasks × 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = **68.78%**). All three candidates exceed this baseline by 1.3–3 points; **GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS**. We are adopting it as the new RACE evaluator (with **GPT-5.4-mini** for the FACT pipeline). Scores:
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:20:  | **GPT-5.5** 🥇 | **71.82** | **73.00** | 89.70 | 65.35 | **59.23** |
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:24:+ [11 May 2026] 📢 **Leaderboard Migration Plan**:
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:26:  > - **Now – 31 May 2026 (dual-acceptance window)**: We accept submissions evaluated under **both** the legacy evaluator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) **and** the new one (GPT-5.5). Results are displayed on **two separate leaderboards** so the rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator.
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:27:  > - **By 1 June 2026 (full migration)**: For the results reported in the original DRB paper, we will re-evaluate them under GPT-5.5 and migrate them to the new leaderboard automatically. For prior community submissions evaluated under Gemini-2.5-Pro, if you would like to keep your entry on the new leaderboard, please contact us per [Submit to Leaderboard](#submit-to-leaderboard) and re-submit following the updated requirements. New submitters: follow the keys / config in [API Configuration](#api-configuration) below. After 1 June, Gemini-2.5-Pro acceptance ends and only the GPT-5.5 leaderboard is maintained going forward.
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:28:  > - **GPT-5.5 leaderboard status**: still under construction — expected to launch within a week, alongside the migrated scores.
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:31:+ [11 May 2026] 🔧 **Evaluation Pipeline v2**: In the new release we have refined the article-cleaning logic, using a chunk-based strategy to better support very long articles.
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:33:+ [6 Feb 2026] 🚀 **DeepResearch Bench II Release**: We have released **DeepResearch Bench II (DRB II)** ([homepage](https://agentresearchlab.org/benchmarks/deepresearch-bench-ii/index.html#home)｜[repo](https://github.com/imlrz/DeepResearch-Bench-II)｜[paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.08536)). We welcome you to evaluate and exchange ideas. Note that DRB II, as a follow-up to DRB, has a different evaluation focus from DRB; **DRB will continue to be maintained and updated** after the release of DRB II. For more details, please refer to the [DRB II paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.08536).
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:35:+ [6 Feb 2026] 📚 **New Papers from Our Lab**: We welcome you to check out the new papers from our lab ([Agent Research Lab](https://agentresearchlab.org/index.html)):
/tmp/drbrepo/README.md:38:    - [Wiki Live Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.01590): A live benchmark that uses Wikipedia Good Articles as expert-level references, with fine-grained criteria for writing quality and factual verifiability.

cd /tmp && for f in $(python3 - <<'EOF'
import json,urllib.request
u="https://huggingface.co/api/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard"
d=json.load(urllib.request.urlopen(u))
print(" ".join(s["rfilename"] for s in d.get("siblings",[])))
EOF
); do echo "$f"; done 2>&1 | head -30

cd /tmp && curl -sL "https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv" -o lb55.csv && head -1 lb55.csv && grep -i gemini lb55.csv; echo "=== N systems ==="; wc -l < lb55.csv; wc -l < lb.csv; echo "=== insight rank of gemini in gpt55 board ==="; python3 - <<'EOF'
import csv
for name,f in [("gpt5.5","/tmp/lb55.csv"),("legacy","/tmp/lb.csv")]:
    rows=list(csv.DictReader(open(f)))
    rows=[r for r in rows if r.get("insight") not in (None,"","-")]
    rows.sort(key=lambda r: float(r["insight"]))
    ins=[r["model"] for r in rows]
    g=[i for i,m in enumerate(ins) if "gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch" in m]
    print(name, "n=",len(rows), "gemini insight rank from bottom:", [i+1 for i in g], "of", len(rows))
    print("  lowest-5 insight:", [(r["model"],r["insight"]) for r in rows[:5]])
EOF

content.text: Both leaderboard numbers confirmed exactly. Let me test the sub-claim that the reference is the "weakest-insight report among all >50 systems".

input.command:
cd /tmp && python3 - <<'EOF'
import csv, json, statistics as st, re
rows=list(csv.DictReader(open('/tmp/lb.csv')))
print("legacy board entries:", len(rows))
ins=[(float(r['insight']), r['model']) for r in rows if r['insight'] not in ('','-')]
ins.sort(reverse=True)
g=[i for i,(v,m) in enumerate(ins) if m=='gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch'][0]
print(f"gemini insight rank {g+1} of {len(ins)} (1=best); value {ins[g][0]}")
print("systems with LOWER insight than reference:", sum(1 for v,m in ins if v< ins[g][0]))
# structural stats of the shipped reference corpus
ref=[json.loads(l) for l in open('/tmp/drbrepo/data/test_data/raw_data/reference.jsonl')]
print("reference corpus n =", len(ref), "keys:", list(ref[0].keys()))
arts=[r.get('article','') for r in ref]
def stats(a):
    paras=[p for p in a.split('\n\n') if p.strip()]
    return (len(paras), len(a.split()), len(re.findall(r'^### ',a,re.M)), a.count('\n|'), len(re.findall(r'^\s*[-*] ',a,re.M)))
S=[stats(a) for a in arts]
for i,lbl in enumerate(['paras','words','H3','table-lines','bullets']):
    print(f"  median {lbl}: {st.median(x[i] for x in S):.0f}")
EOF

content.text: Everything checks out on the primary source. Let me record the verdict.

input.reasoning:
CORE CLAIM: CONFIRMED from primary source, and still CURRENT in 2026.

1) The quote is real and verbatim. I downloaded the paper PDF (arXiv 2506.11763, the same doc served at deepresearch-bench.github.io/static/papers/deepresearch-bench.pdf) and extracted text with PyMuPDF. Section 4.1 Experimental Setup / Implementation Details reads exactly: "For RACE evaluation tasks, we utilize Gemini-2.5-pro as the Judge LLM. As for the FACT framework, Gemini-2.5-flash is employed... The reference reports (Rref) used in RACE's scoring methodology were selected from deep research articles generated by the Gemini-2.5-pro-based Deep Research, as available in April 2025[3]." Footnote 3: "We cannot confirm if there were any Deep Research model iterations during the reference collection period." Nowhere does the paper claim human-authored references. Humans wrote the 100 *tasks* (PhD-level, 22 fields) and the human-annotation subset used to validate the judge — that is likely the origin of the "human reference" confusion. The reference *reports* are machine-generated.

2) Score formula makes 0.5000 structural: Eq. 3 is Sfinal(Rtgt) = Sint(Rtgt) / (Sint(Rtgt) + Sint(Rref)). Reference vs. a sibling sample of itself → ~0.50. Confirmed.

3) Both corroborating leaderboard numbers verified EXACTLY, pulled from the live HF Space (muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard, lastModified 2026-06-28):
   - data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv → gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch,49.98 (comp 50.01, insight 49.92, inst 50.22, read 49.58)
   - data/leaderboard.csv (legacy) → gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch,49.71 (comp 49.51, insight 49.45, inst 50.12, read 50.00)

4) CURRENCY CHECK — this is the part that could have killed the claim, and it survives. The evaluator DID change (repo README, 11 May 2026: official RACE evaluator switched Gemini-2.5-Pro → GPT-5.5, FACT → GPT-5.4-mini, ahead of Gemini-2.5-Pro's 17 June 2026 deprecation; commit 899d818). But the JUDGE changing does not touch the REFERENCE. I cloned the full history: data/test_data/raw_data/reference.jsonl and .../cleaned_data/reference.jsonl have exactly ONE commit each — 214741e "feat: initial release of DeepResearch Bench v1.0". The reference corpus has never been modified. So the April-2025 Gemini-2.5-pro DR reference is still the denominator under the GPT-5.5 judge — which is precisely why the reference still lands at 49.98 on the new board. The premise-busting holds under the current evaluator.

ONE SUB-CLAIM IS FALSE (see `corrected`): "it is the weakest-insight report among all >50 systems." It is not. On the legacy leaderboard (45 entries, not >50) gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch ranks 27th of 45 on insight (49.45); 18 systems score LOWER on insight. It is mid-pack, not the floor. The reference is beaten on insight by ~26 systems, which is the actually useful framing: insight is the dimension where the reference is most beatable, but calling it "the weakest" overstates it and is the kind of error that would mislead a POLARIS strategy that leans on it.

Minor: the structural fingerprint (59 paragraphs / 142 wpp / 24 H3 / 10 tables / 91 bullets) is POLARIS-internal, not from the paper. Spot-checking the shipped 100-article reference corpus gives medians of ~30 paragraphs, 6,432 words, 16 H3, 94 bullets — bullets match well, the rest look like means over a different segmentation. Not load-bearing for the verdict, but do not cite those numbers as if the paper states them.

Files: paper text at /tmp/drb.txt; repo at /tmp/drbfull; leaderboards at /tmp/lb.csv and /tmp/lb55.csv.
input.corrected: The main claim needs no correction. The one embedded error: the RACE reference is NOT "the weakest-insight report among all >50 systems." Source: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data/leaderboard.csv — the legacy board has 45 entries (not >50), and gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch's insight score of 49.45 ranks 27th of 45, with 18 systems scoring lower. Correct statement: insight is the dimension the reference is most widely beaten on (26 systems exceed it), and the top of the board reaches ~59-61 insight (e.g. qianfan_deepresearch_0430: 61.48) — so there is real headroom, but the reference is mid-pack, not the floor.
